title: Arts and Culture
overview: Britain has a rich and varied arts and cultural heritage spanning music, theatre, art, architecture, literature and poetry. Many of the world's most famous artists, composers, architects and authors are British. This lesson covers the key figures and achievements in British arts and culture that you need to know for the Life in the UK test.

study_sections.heading: Classical Music
study_sections.content:
The Proms is an eight-week summer season of orchestral classical music, including at the Royal Albert Hall in London. It has been organised by the BBC since 1927. The Last Night of the Proms is the most well-known concert.

Henry Purcell (1659-95) was the organist at Westminster Abbey. He wrote church music, operas and other pieces, and developed a distinct British style.

George Frederick Handel (1695-1759) was German-born but spent many years in the UK and became a British citizen in 1727. He wrote the Water Music for King George I, Music for the Royal Fireworks for George II, and the oratorio Messiah, which is sung regularly by choirs, often at Easter.

Gustav Holst (1874-1934) wrote The Planets suite. He adapted Jupiter as the tune for 'I vow to thee my country'.

Sir Edward Elgar (1857-1934) was born in Worcester. His best-known work is the Pomp and Circumstance Marches. March No 1 (Land of Hope and Glory) is usually played at the Last Night of the Proms.

Ralph Vaughan Williams (1872-1958) wrote music for orchestras and choirs, strongly influenced by traditional English folk music.

Benjamin Britten (1913-76) is best known for his operas Peter Grimes and Billy Budd. He also wrote A Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra. He founded the Aldeburgh festival in Suffolk.

study_sections.heading: Pop Music and Festivals
study_sections.content:
Britain has had a huge impact on popular music around the world. Since the 1960s, British pop music has made one of the most important cultural contributions to life in the UK. Bands including The Beatles and The Rolling Stones continue to have an influence on music both in the UK and abroad. British pop music has continued to innovate - for example, the Punk movement of the late 1970s, and boy and girl bands in the 1990s.

Famous music festivals include Glastonbury, the Isle of Wight Festival and the V Festival.

The National Eisteddfod of Wales is an annual cultural festival which includes music, dance, art and original performances largely in Welsh.

The Mercury Music Prize is awarded each September for the best album from the UK and Ireland. The Brit Awards gives awards in categories such as best British group and best British solo artist.

study_sections.heading: Theatre
study_sections.content:
London's West End, also known as 'Theatreland', is particularly well known. The Mousetrap, a murder-mystery play by Dame Agatha Christie, has been running in the West End since 1952 and has had the longest initial run of any show in history.

Gilbert and Sullivan wrote comic operas in the 19th century, including HMS Pinafore, The Pirates of Penzance and The Mikado. Andrew Lloyd Webber wrote the music for shows including Jesus Christ Superstar, Evita, Cats and The Phantom of the Opera.

One British tradition is the pantomime. Many theatres produce a pantomime at Christmas time, based on fairy stories with music and comedy for family audiences.

The Edinburgh Festival takes place every summer. The biggest part is the Edinburgh Festival Fringe (the Fringe), which showcases mainly theatre and comedy performances.

The Laurence Olivier Awards take place annually in London across various categories including best director, best actor and best actress. They are named after Sir Laurence Olivier.

study_sections.heading: Art and Architecture
study_sections.content:
Notable British artists include:
• Thomas Gainsborough (1727-88) - portrait painter
• Joseph Turner (1775-1851) - influential landscape painter, after whom the Turner Prize is named
• John Constable (1776-1837) - landscape painter known for Dedham Vale
• Henry Moore (1898-1986) - large bronze abstract sculptures
• David Hockney (1937-) - important contributor to the 'pop art' movement of the 1960s

The Turner Prize was established in 1984 and celebrates contemporary art. Four works are shortlisted every year and shown at Tate Britain before the winner is announced.

Well-known galleries include The National Gallery, Tate Britain and Tate Modern in London, the National Museum in Cardiff, and the National Gallery of Scotland in Edinburgh.

In architecture, Inigo Jones designed the Queen's House at Greenwich and the Banqueting House in Whitehall. Sir Christopher Wren designed the new St Paul's Cathedral. Robert Adam influenced architecture in the UK, Europe and America in the 18th century. Sir Edwin Lutyens designed the Cenotaph in Whitehall and war memorials throughout the world.

Modern British architects Sir Norman Foster, Lord Richard Rogers and Dame Zaha Hadid continue to work on major projects worldwide.

Lancelot 'Capability' Brown designed the grounds around country houses in the 18th century. The annual Chelsea Flower Show showcases garden design.

study_sections.heading: Literature
study_sections.content:
Notable British authors include:
• Jane Austen (1775-1817) - Pride and Prejudice, Sense and Sensibility
• Charles Dickens (1812-70) - Oliver Twist, Great Expectations
• Robert Louis Stevenson (1850-94) - Treasure Island, Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde
• Sir Arthur Conan Doyle (1859-1930) - Sherlock Holmes stories
• Thomas Hardy (1840-1928) - Far from the Madding Crowd, Jude the Obscure
• Graham Greene (1904-91) - Brighton Rock, Our Man in Havana
• Evelyn Waugh (1903-66) - Brideshead Revisited
• J K Rowling (1965-) - Harry Potter series

Several British writers have won the Nobel Prize in Literature, including Sir William Golding (novelist), Seamus Heaney (poet) and Harold Pinter (playwright).

The Man Booker Prize is awarded annually for the best fiction novel by an author from the Commonwealth, Ireland or Zimbabwe. It has been awarded since 1968. Past winners include Ian McEwan, Hilary Mantel and Julian Barnes.

In 2003, The Lord of the Rings by JRR Tolkien was voted the country's best-loved novel.

study_sections.heading: Poetry
study_sections.content:
British poetry is among the richest in the world. The Anglo-Saxon poem Beowulf is still translated today. John Milton wrote Paradise Lost. William Wordsworth, William Blake, Lord Byron, John Keats and Percy Shelley were important 19th century poets. Wilfred Owen and Siegfried Sassoon wrote about their experiences in the First World War.

Famous poetic lines include:
• 'I wander'd lonely as a cloud... A host of golden daffodils' (Wordsworth - The Daffodils)
• 'She walks in beauty, like the night...' (Byron - She Walks in Beauty)
• 'Tyger! Tyger! Burning bright...' (Blake - The Tyger)
• 'What passing-bells for these who die as cattle...' (Wilfred Owen - Anthem for Doomed Youth)
• 'Oh to be in England now that April's there...' (Robert Browning - Home Thoughts from Abroad)

Some of the best-known poets are buried or commemorated in Poet's Corner in Westminster Abbey.

Henry Purcell (1659-95) was organist at Westminster Abbey.
Handel became a British citizen in 1727.
Handel wrote the Water Music and Music for the Royal Fireworks.
Handel's Messiah is sung regularly by choirs, often at Easter.
Holst wrote The Planets; Jupiter became the tune for 'I vow to thee my country'.
Elgar's Land of Hope and Glory is played at the Last Night of the Proms.
Benjamin Britten founded the Aldeburgh festival in Suffolk.
The Beatles and Rolling Stones continue to influence music worldwide.
The Mousetrap by Agatha Christie has been running in London's West End since 1952.
Gilbert and Sullivan wrote HMS Pinafore, The Pirates of Penzance and The Mikado.
Andrew Lloyd Webber wrote Cats, The Phantom of the Opera, Evita and Jesus Christ Superstar.
The Edinburgh Festival Fringe (the Fringe) showcases theatre and comedy.
The Laurence Olivier Awards are named after Sir Laurence Olivier.
The Turner Prize was established in 1984.
The Turner Prize was named after Joseph Turner, the landscape painter.
Shortlisted works are shown at Tate Britain before the winner is announced.
Sir Christopher Wren designed St Paul's Cathedral.
Sir Edwin Lutyens designed the Cenotaph in Whitehall.
Robert Adam influenced architecture in the UK, Europe and America.
Jane Austen wrote Pride and Prejudice and Sense and Sensibility.
Charles Dickens wrote Oliver Twist and Great Expectations.
Sir Arthur Conan Doyle created Sherlock Holmes.
JK Rowling wrote the Harry Potter series.
The Lord of the Rings by JRR Tolkien was voted best-loved novel in 2003.
The Man Booker Prize has been awarded since 1968.

Poet's Corner is in Westminster Abbey.
Nick Park has won four Oscars for animated films including Wallace and Gromit.
memory_hook: For classical composers remember birth order: Purcell (1659), Handel (1695), Elgar (1857), Holst (1874), Vaughan Williams (1872), Britten (1913). For authors: Austen, Dickens, Doyle, Hardy, Waugh/Greene, Rowling - from Regency to modern. Turner Prize = Turner the painter, shown at Tate Britain.

questions.prompt: Who has organised the Proms since 1927?
questions.explanation: The Proms has been organised by the BBC since 1927.

questions.prompt: Where was Henry Purcell the organist?
questions.explanation: Henry Purcell (1659-95) was the organist at Westminster Abbey.

questions.prompt: When did Handel become a British citizen?
questions.explanation: The German-born composer Handel spent many years in the UK and became a British citizen in 1727.

questions.prompt: What did Handel write that is sung at Easter?
questions.explanation: Handel wrote the oratorio Messiah, which is sung regularly by choirs, often at Easter time.

questions.prompt: What is Gustav Holst best known for?
questions.explanation: Gustav Holst wrote The Planets, a suite of pieces themed around the planets. Jupiter became 'I vow to thee my country'.

questions.prompt: What is usually played at the Last Night of the Proms?
options: Land of Hope and Glory by Elgar
questions.explanation: Elgar's Pomp and Circumstance March No 1 (Land of Hope and Glory) is usually played at the Last Night of the Proms.

questions.prompt: What festival did Benjamin Britten found?
questions.explanation: Benjamin Britten founded the Aldeburgh festival in Suffolk.

questions.prompt: How long has The Mousetrap been running in the West End?
questions.explanation: The Mousetrap by Agatha Christie has been running in the West End since 1952.

questions.prompt: Who wrote The Mousetrap?
questions.explanation: The Mousetrap is a murder-mystery play by Dame Agatha Christie.

questions.prompt: Which shows did Andrew Lloyd Webber write?
options: Cats and The Phantom of the Opera
questions.explanation: Andrew Lloyd Webber wrote Cats, The Phantom of the Opera, Jesus Christ Superstar and Evita.

questions.prompt: What is the Edinburgh Festival Fringe known for?
questions.explanation: The Edinburgh Festival Fringe showcases mainly theatre and comedy performances.

questions.prompt: Who are the Laurence Olivier Awards named after?
questions.explanation: The awards are named after the British actor Sir Laurence Olivier, known for his roles in Shakespeare plays.

questions.prompt: When was the Turner Prize established?
questions.explanation: The Turner Prize was established in 1984.

questions.prompt: Who was the Turner Prize named after?
options: J M W Turner, the politician
questions.explanation: The Turner Prize was named after Joseph Turner (1775-1851), the influential landscape painter.

questions.prompt: Where are Turner Prize shortlisted works shown?
questions.explanation: Four works are shortlisted every year and shown at Tate Britain before the winner is announced.

questions.prompt: Who designed the Cenotaph in Whitehall?
questions.explanation: Sir Edwin Lutyens was responsible for many war memorials, including the Cenotaph in Whitehall.

questions.prompt: What did Jane Austen write?
options: Pride and Prejudice and Sense and Sensibility
Treasure Island and Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde
questions.explanation: Jane Austen (1775-1817) wrote Pride and Prejudice and Sense and Sensibility.

questions.prompt: Who wrote Sherlock Holmes?
questions.explanation: Sir Arthur Conan Doyle (1859-1930) was best known for his Sherlock Holmes stories.

questions.prompt: Who wrote the Harry Potter series?
questions.explanation: JK Rowling (1965-) wrote the Harry Potter series of children's books.

questions.prompt: Which novel was voted best-loved in the UK in 2003?
options: The Lord of the Rings by JRR Tolkien
Pride and Prejudice by Jane Austen
questions.explanation: In 2003, The Lord of the Rings by JRR Tolkien was voted the country's best-loved novel.

questions.prompt: When was the Man Booker Prize first awarded?
questions.explanation: The Man Booker Prize has been awarded since 1968.

questions.prompt: Who wrote Anthem for Doomed Youth?
questions.explanation: Wilfred Owen (1893-1918) wrote Anthem for Doomed Youth, inspired by the First World War.

questions.prompt: Where is Poet's Corner?
questions.explanation: Some of the best-known poets are buried or commemorated in Poet's Corner in Westminster Abbey.

questions.prompt: How many Oscars has Nick Park won for animation?
options: Four, including three for Wallace and Gromit films
questions.explanation: Nick Park has won four Oscars for his animated films, including three for films featuring Wallace and Gromit.
